question: A developer writes: save the order to the database, then publish an 'orderCreated' event to the broker, with no outbox or CDC. What is the fundamental flaw, known as the dual-write problem?

choices.text: The two writes target different systems with no shared transaction, so a crash or failure between them can leave the DB updated but the event unpublished (or vice versa).
The two operations are too slow because they run sequentially instead of in parallel.
The broker will reject the message because the order does not yet exist in its own storage.
Publishing after saving guarantees ordering problems but never data-loss problems.

explanations.a: Correct. The dual-write problem is the lack of atomicity across two independent systems (database and broker). If the process crashes, or the publish fails after the commit succeeds, the two sources of truth diverge: the order exists but no one was notified — or, if reordered, an event fires for an order that was never persisted.
explanations.b: Wrong. Performance/sequencing is not the issue; even if both were fast, the atomicity gap remains. The defect is correctness, not latency.
explanations.c: Wrong. Brokers do not require the order to exist in the database; they accept the message regardless. The flaw is the absence of a shared transaction, not broker-side validation.
explanations.d: Wrong. The dual-write problem absolutely can cause data/notification loss (the inconsistency), not merely ordering issues. Reordering the two writes only moves which side can be left orphaned.

question: A purely choreographed saga across six services is working but operations complain they cannot tell 'where an order is' in the flow. Which statement best captures the inherent limitation being hit?

choices.text: Choreography distributes the workflow logic across services with no single owner, so end-to-end visibility and cyclic-dependency risk become harder to manage as the flow grows.
Choreography is impossible to monitor at all because events are not persisted anywhere.
Choreography requires a central orchestrator by definition, so the complaint means it was implemented incorrectly.
Choreography guarantees strong consistency, so the only possible issue is database performance.

explanations.a: Correct. In choreography each service reacts to events and emits its own, so the overall process is implicit and emergent rather than written down in one place. This buys loose coupling and autonomy but makes the end-to-end flow hard to see, reason about, and debug, and raises the risk of tangled/cyclic event dependencies as participants multiply.
explanations.b: Wrong. The events can be logged, traced (e.g., correlation IDs/distributed tracing), and audited; visibility is harder but not impossible. The limitation is the lack of a single place that owns the flow, not absence of data.
explanations.c: Wrong. Choreography by definition has NO central orchestrator — that is orchestration. The complaint reflects a known trade-off of choreography, not a misimplementation.
explanations.d: Wrong. Choreographed sagas are eventually consistent, not strongly consistent. The visibility problem is about workflow observability, not database performance.

question: A UI submits a command whose effect (a new comment) only appears in the read model after async projection. To make the app feel responsive despite this lag, the most common UX pattern is:

choices.text: Optimistic UI: render the comment locally immediately on command acceptance, then reconcile with the read model when the projection catches up.
Block the UI with a spinner until the read model is provably up to date for all users globally.
Make the command synchronous by writing directly into the read model and skipping the write model entirely.
Disable the comment feature until the system can offer strong consistency.

explanations.a: Correct. Optimistic UI assumes the accepted command will succeed and immediately reflects it in the local view, hiding projection lag from the user. When the read model catches up, the client reconciles; if the command is later rejected, the UI rolls back. This is the standard way to deliver responsiveness on top of eventual consistency.
explanations.b: Wrong. Blocking until the read model is globally up to date defeats the scalability of async projections and produces a sluggish experience — exactly what eventual-consistency UX patterns aim to avoid.
explanations.c: Wrong. Writing directly into the read model bypasses the authoritative write model and its invariants, undermining the CQRS separation and risking inconsistency between the two sides.
explanations.d: Wrong. Eventual consistency does not require disabling the feature; UX patterns like optimistic rendering and read-your-writes make it perfectly usable without strong consistency.

question: A CQRS read model is denormalized so a dashboard query hits one pre-joined table instead of joining five normalized tables at request time. What is the main cost of this design choice?

choices.text: Data is duplicated and must be kept in sync by projections, so the read model is eventually consistent and updates cost extra write/maintenance work.
Query latency increases because denormalized tables are always larger and slower to scan than normalized joins.
The write model loses its ability to enforce invariants, since denormalization removes all constraints.
Denormalized read models cannot be rebuilt and must be migrated manually whenever the schema changes.

explanations.a: Correct. Denormalization trades cheap reads for redundant data that the system must actively keep consistent. Projections must update the read model whenever source events occur, which adds write-path and maintenance cost and means the read side is eventually consistent with the write side — the classic read-optimized trade-off.
explanations.b: Wrong. The whole point is that pre-joined, read-optimized tables make queries FASTER by avoiding expensive runtime joins; latency typically drops, not rises. Size alone does not make a targeted lookup slower than a five-table join.
explanations.c: Wrong. Invariant enforcement lives on the write model, which is unaffected by how the read side is shaped. Denormalizing the query side does not strip the write side of its constraints.
explanations.d: Wrong. Denormalized read models are usually easy to rebuild — you can drop and replay/regenerate them from the source of truth (events or the write store), which is a key advantage, not a limitation.

question: A team runs Lambda Architecture: a batch layer recomputes views from the full dataset nightly while a speed layer serves real-time approximations, and queries merge both. They want to move to Kappa Architecture. What is the defining structural change Kappa makes?

choices.text: Eliminate the separate batch layer and treat the immutable log as the single source of truth, reprocessing history by replaying the stream through the same streaming code.
Eliminate the speed layer so all queries are served exclusively from precomputed batch views, improving accuracy.
Replace the append-only log with a mutable, in-place-updated database to avoid storing full history.
Keep both layers but move the batch layer onto the same cluster as the speed layer to reduce hardware cost.

explanations.a: Correct. Kappa's core idea is one code path: a single stream-processing layer reads from a durable, replayable log. Reprocessing (e.g., after a logic change) is done by replaying the retained log through the same streaming job, removing the dual-codebase maintenance burden of Lambda's separate batch layer.
explanations.b: Backwards. Kappa removes the batch layer, not the speed layer; the streaming layer becomes the only layer. Removing the real-time path would defeat the goal of low-latency serving.
explanations.c: Wrong. Kappa depends on an immutable, append-only, replayable log (e.g., Kafka with long retention). A mutable in-place store would destroy the ability to reprocess history by replay, which is exactly how Kappa substitutes for batch recomputation.
explanations.d: Co-locating Lambda's two layers is an ops tweak, not Kappa. Kappa's value is collapsing two code paths into one, eliminating the dual-implementation drift, not consolidating hardware while keeping both layers.

question: When converting a synchronous request-driven call (Order service calls Inventory service via REST and waits) into an event-driven flow (Order emits an OrderPlaced event consumed asynchronously by Inventory), which consequence is intrinsic to that change?

choices.text: The producer no longer learns the outcome within the original call, so downstream failures must be handled out-of-band and the overall flow becomes eventually consistent.
The two services become temporally and behaviorally coupled, since the producer now blocks until every consumer acknowledges.
Strong, immediate consistency between Order and Inventory is guaranteed because the broker commits both writes in one distributed transaction.
The producer must know the network address and API contract of each consumer in advance, increasing point-to-point coupling.

explanations.a: Correct. Fire-and-forget eventing decouples producer from consumer in time, but the producer no longer gets a synchronous success/failure. Errors surface later and are handled via retries, DLQs, or compensation, and the system is eventually consistent rather than immediately consistent.
explanations.b: Inverted. Event-driven design reduces temporal coupling: the producer does not block waiting for consumers. Blocking-until-ack describes the synchronous request-driven model it is replacing.
explanations.c: A broker does not enroll the producer's DB write and the consumer's DB write in one distributed ACID transaction. Cross-service atomicity is precisely what you lose; you get eventual consistency and patterns like the outbox to bridge the gap.
explanations.d: This describes request-driven point-to-point coupling. Publishing to a topic/broker means the producer does not need to know consumer addresses or contracts, which is one of the decoupling benefits gained, not lost.

question: A SaaS platform adopts cell-based architecture: independent, self-contained 'cells' (each a full stack serving a subset of users) sit behind a thin cell router. What is the primary resilience property this is designed to provide?

diagram.alt: A cell router directing different user groups to isolated cells, each containing its own application and data stores.
diagram.source:
graph LR
  R[Cell Router] --> C1[Cell 1 app and data]
  R --> C2[Cell 2 app and data]
  R --> C3[Cell 3 app and data]

choices.text: Blast-radius containment: a failure or bad deploy is bounded to the cell where it occurs, so it cannot cascade across the entire user base.
Stronger global consistency, because all cells share one primary database that the router keeps synchronized.
Lower total infrastructure cost, since cells let many tenants share a single pooled application instance.
Elimination of the need for load balancing, because the router statically pins every request to one fixed cell forever.

explanations.a: Correct. The defining benefit of cells is fault isolation: each cell is independent (own compute and data), so an outage, poison input, or bad rollout is contained to that cell's user subset. Deploys can also be rolled cell-by-cell to limit risk.
explanations.b: Wrong. Cells deliberately do NOT share one primary database; each is self-contained. A shared global DB would reintroduce a single point of failure and defeat blast-radius isolation.
explanations.c: Cells trade some efficiency (duplicated stacks per cell) for isolation; pooling many tenants into one shared instance is the opposite resource-sharing model. Cost reduction is not the goal.
explanations.d: The router does map users to cells, but cells still need internal load balancing, and mappings can be rebalanced (e.g., to drain or migrate a cell). 'Static forever' is not a requirement and misstates the model.

question: A B2B SaaS must choose a multi-tenancy data model. Which characterization of the 'database-per-tenant' (silo) model versus a 'shared schema with tenant_id column' (pool) model is correct?

choices.text: Database-per-tenant maximizes isolation and simplifies per-tenant backup/restore and noisy-neighbor control, but scales worse in cost/operational overhead as tenant count grows.
Shared-schema with tenant_id gives the strongest isolation because each query physically separates tenant data into different files.
Database-per-tenant removes the need to ever filter by tenant in application code and is therefore always the cheapest option at scale.
Shared-schema is impossible to operate safely because a missing WHERE tenant_id clause can never be mitigated by any technical control.

explanations.a: Correct. Silo (DB-per-tenant) gives strong isolation, easy per-tenant restore, and natural blast-radius/noisy-neighbor limits, at the cost of managing many databases, which becomes expensive and operationally heavy at thousands of tenants. Pool (shared schema) inverts these trade-offs.
explanations.b: Wrong. Shared-schema co-mingles tenants in the same tables; isolation is logical (enforced by tenant_id filtering / row-level security), not physical file separation. It is the weaker-isolation model.
explanations.c: Partly true that you avoid tenant filtering, but it is NOT cheapest at scale; the per-database operational and licensing overhead grows linearly and typically makes silo more expensive than pool for large tenant counts.
explanations.d: Overstated. Shared-schema risk from a forgotten filter is real but mitigable, e.g., Postgres row-level security, query builders that always inject tenant_id, or middleware. Saying no control can help is incorrect.

question: In a blue-green deployment, the new version (green) is brought up alongside the live version (blue), and traffic is switched over via the router/load balancer. Compared with a canary release, what distinguishes blue-green?

choices.text: Blue-green flips effectively all traffic from old to new at the cutover point and enables fast rollback by switching back, whereas canary shifts a small percentage first and ramps gradually.
Blue-green exposes the new version to a small percentage of users first and increases it slowly, whereas canary swaps everyone at once.
Blue-green guarantees zero database schema concerns because the two environments never share any state.
Blue-green requires no second environment, since the new version is deployed in place over the old one.

explanations.a: Correct. Blue-green keeps two full environments and cuts traffic over at once (all-or-nothing flip), with rollback = switch back to blue. Canary instead routes a small slice to the new version and ramps up while watching metrics. That gradual-vs-atomic distinction is the key difference.
explanations.b: Reversed. The 'small percentage first, ramp slowly' description is canary; the 'swap everyone at once' is blue-green. The choice swaps the two definitions.
explanations.c: Wrong. Blue and green typically share the same production database, so backward/forward-compatible schema changes (expand-contract migrations) are a real and central concern in blue-green, not eliminated.
explanations.d: That describes an in-place/recreate deploy. Blue-green's whole premise is two parallel environments so you can cut over and roll back by switching the router.

question: A team enables a canary release: 2% of production traffic goes to the new version while automated checks compare its metrics against the stable baseline. What is the central purpose of doing this with live traffic rather than relying only on pre-prod testing?

choices.text: To limit the blast radius while validating the new version against real production conditions, so problems are detected on a small slice before a full rollout.
To permanently split load across two versions, since running old and new code side by side is the steady-state goal of canarying.
To prove the new version is correct, since exposing it to a sample of real users exercises every code path.
To replace the need for staging environments, because production traffic alone fully substitutes for pre-release testing.

explanations.a: Correct. Canary's value is risk reduction under realistic load and data: a tiny fraction of users is exposed, metrics (errors, latency, business KPIs) are compared to baseline, and you promote or roll back based on evidence, containing the blast radius if the release is bad.
explanations.b: Wrong. Canary is a temporary, asymmetric split for validation, not a permanent load-balancing strategy across divergent code. The new version is meant to fully replace the old once validated.
explanations.c: Overclaim. A 2% canary reduces risk but proves nothing about absolute correctness; rare paths may never be hit by a small live sample. It surfaces problems early, it does not guarantee bug-freedom.
explanations.d: Wrong. Canary complements pre-prod testing rather than replacing it; you still need staging and automated tests, plus observability to compare canary metrics against the baseline and decide promote-vs-rollback.

question: A team must replace a legacy persistence library used throughout the codebase, but wants to keep shipping to trunk daily without a long-lived feature branch. They apply branch-by-abstraction. What is the correct sequence of the technique?

choices.text: Introduce an abstraction layer over the old implementation, migrate callers to the abstraction, build the new implementation behind it, switch the abstraction to the new implementation, then remove the old one.
Cut a long-lived branch, rewrite the persistence layer fully there, and merge once it is completely done.
Delete the old implementation first, leave callers broken, then race to write the new one before the next release.
Duplicate the entire application into a parallel deployment and route half of users to the new persistence code via DNS.

explanations.a: Correct. Branch-by-abstraction works in trunk: (1) insert an abstraction over the existing code, (2) point all clients at it, (3) implement the replacement behind the same abstraction, (4) flip clients/abstraction to the new implementation incrementally, (5) delete the old code and optionally the abstraction. This keeps the system shippable throughout.
explanations.b: That is exactly the long-lived-branch approach branch-by-abstraction is meant to avoid; it reintroduces painful big-bang merges and integration drift.
explanations.c: Wrong and unsafe. The technique never leaves the build broken; the abstraction keeps the old implementation working until the new one is ready and switched in. Deleting first defeats the purpose.
explanations.d: That describes blue-green or parallel-run deployment routing, a runtime traffic-splitting concern. Branch-by-abstraction is a source-level refactoring strategy, not a deployment/routing strategy.

question: The Twelve-Factor App methodology's Config factor (III) states that config which varies between deploys must be stored in the environment. Which practice does this factor specifically prohibit?

choices.text: Hardcoding per-deploy values (like database credentials or API keys) as constants committed in the source code or in language-specific config files baked into the build.
Reading the database URL from an environment variable at startup.
Running multiple deploys (staging, prod) from the same build artifact with different env values.
Treating backing services such as the database as attached resources referenced by a URL in config.

explanations.a: Correct. Factor III's litmus test: could you open-source the repo without leaking secrets? Config that varies per deploy (credentials, hostnames, keys) must live in the environment, not as constants in code or in checked-in, per-environment config files. That is precisely what it prohibits.
explanations.b: Allowed and encouraged. Reading config from environment variables at runtime is the prescribed way to satisfy Factor III, not a violation.
explanations.c: This is the desired outcome of separating config from code: one immutable build promoted across deploys, parameterized by env. It is compliant, not prohibited.
explanations.d: That is Factor IV (Backing Services), and it is a recommended practice. Referencing a DB by a config URL aligns with twelve-factor; it is not what Factor III forbids.

question: A latency-sensitive API is moved to a FaaS platform (e.g., AWS Lambda) where each function instance is stateless and ephemeral. Which tradeoff is genuinely inherent to this FaaS model?

choices.text: Cold starts: an idle/scaled-to-zero function must spin up a fresh instance on a new request, adding initialization latency, and you cannot rely on in-process state persisting between invocations.
Functions can hold long-lived in-memory caches and open connection pools that are guaranteed to persist for the application's lifetime.
FaaS removes all concurrency limits, so a single function scales to unbounded parallelism with no downstream impact.
Because billing is per-request, FaaS is always cheaper than a provisioned server regardless of sustained traffic volume.

explanations.a: Correct. FaaS instances are ephemeral and can scale to zero, so requests hitting a cold instance pay initialization latency (runtime + dependencies + your init). And because instances are short-lived and not under your control, you cannot treat in-process memory as durable state, you must externalize it.
explanations.b: Wrong. Instances are reused opportunistically but not guaranteed; warm-instance caches/pools can vanish at any time when instances are recycled. You cannot rely on lifetime persistence; this is the warm-state pitfall.
explanations.c: Overstated. Platforms impose concurrency limits, and high fan-out can overwhelm downstream databases (connection storms). Scaling is high but neither unbounded nor free of downstream consequences.
explanations.d: Not always. Per-request billing favors spiky/low-duty-cycle workloads; for sustained high traffic a provisioned instance is often cheaper. Cost depends on the traffic profile.
